<insights>

- Zone groups tools with specialized command-line interfaces (git, test runners)
- Moderate cohesion (0.5) suggests tools could be more tightly integrated
- Each tool handles its own command parsing and execution logic
- Moderate cohesion (0.5) and coupling (0.5) indicates loosely related tools that could benefit from stronger unifying patterns
- Git tool properly restricts allowed subcommands for security
- Test runner provides automatic detection of test frameworks and scoped execution
- Operates as leaf nodes in tool architecture with minimal cross-dependencies
- Each tool maintains isolated command surface without shared state
- Clean consumer relationship with tool-security zone - accepts validation without coupling to implementation
- Test runner and git tools share no common interface despite similar command-line execution patterns
- Tool implementations lack standardized error handling or result formatting conventions
- Each specialized tool implements its own command parsing and execution without shared abstractions, leading to inconsistent interfaces and duplicated validation logic
- Tools implement divergent error handling strategies - git tool throws exceptions while test-runner returns result objects with error flags
- Command parsing logic duplicated across git.ts and test-runner.ts without shared validation utilities
- Each tool maintains its own subprocess management approach creating inconsistent behavior for timeouts and cancellation
- Extract common command execution interface to standardize error handling and subprocess management across all specialized tools
- Consolidate duplicate argument parsing logic between git.ts and test-runner.ts into shared utility module

</insights>
